message:
fix(scheduler): use a single ticker for worker periodic polling

Scheduler.worker() previously used `case <-time.After(100ms)` for
its periodic "check for work that slipped past the notify channel"
branch.  time.After() allocates a fresh *Timer on every loop
iteration, and that timer is not released until it fires even when
the other branches of the select fire first — which is the common
case when s.jobNotify is active.  Under a busy scheduler with
thousands of job dispatches per second, that produces a steady
stream of leaked timers until GC.  See also the well-known
time.After pitfall documented in the Go runtime code review guide.

Fix:
- Create a single time.NewTicker(100ms) once at the top of worker()
  and `defer ticker.Stop()` so the periodic tick is a fixed-cost
  bookkeeping structure for the lifetime of the worker.
- Replace `case <-time.After(100 * time.Millisecond):` with
  `case <-ticker.C:` so the select arm consumes from the long-lived
  channel instead of allocating per iteration.

No behavioral change to the dispatch logic; the periodic processJobs
call still happens every ~100ms.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -630,14 +630,17 @@ func (s *Scheduler) worker(id int) {
 
 	slog.Debug("worker started", "worker_id", id)
 
+	ticker := time.NewTicker(100 * time.Millisecond)
+	defer ticker.Stop()
+
 	for {
 		select {
 		case <-s.ctx.Done():
 			slog.Debug("worker stopping", "worker_id", id)
 			return
 		case <-s.jobNotify:
 			s.processJobs(id)
-		case <-time.After(100 * time.Millisecond):
+		case <-ticker.C:
 			// Periodic check
 			s.processJobs(id)
 		}
